1 
PURPOSE AND SCOPE  
1.1 SCOPE OF DOCUMENT 
An IPTV (Internet Protocol Television) system installed in an FPSO vessel is a digital television distribution system that 
delivers live TV channels, recorded content, and information services over the vessel’s IP network infrastructure. Unlike 
traditional coaxial TV distribution, IPTV uses the onboard Ethernet/LAN network to transmit video streams from a 
centralized headend to multiple user endpoints such as crew cabins, control rooms, and offices. 
This Installation, Operation, and Maintenance (IOM) Manual provides guidelines for the safe installation, operation, 
and maintenance of the IP TV Radio Communication System installed onboard. The document is intended for use by 
commissioning engineers, operations personnel, and maintenance technicians. 
1.2 SYSTEM DESCRIPTION

1.3 EQUIPMENT DESCRIPTION 
1.3.1 
IPTV / TVRO ANTENNA SEATEL 120TV 
 
The SEATEL 120TV is a high-performance satellite TV reception antenna specifically designed for marine and offshore 
environments, providing reliable reception of television broadcast signals for the vessel’s IPTV / TVRO (Television 
Receive Only) system. Constructed to withstand harsh offshore conditions, this stabilized TV antenna ensures 
consistent and high-quality satellite signal acquisition even with vessel motion and sea state variations. 
The antenna features a precision-engineered reflector and feed assembly optimized for reception in the C-band and 
Ku-band satellite frequencies typically used for direct-to-home (DTH) television transmissions as well as marine 
broadcast services. Its robust mechanical design integrates a stabilized platform that compensates for roll and pitch, 
maintaining accurate satellite alignment and reducing signal loss due to vessel movement.

written consent of Contractor. This document is issued as an “uncontrolled copy” unless over-stamped “CONTROLLED COPY”. 
The SEATEL 120TV operates in conjunction with a TVRO receiver and the onboard IPTV headend, delivering satellite 
television channels to the IPTV distribution system. It connects via coaxial feed cables (50 Ω) to the receiver 
demodulators, which decode the incoming RF satellite signals into digital video streams. These streams are then 
encoded and distributed over the FPSO’s IP network to multiple endpoints such as crew cabins, lounges, and common 
areas. 
 
IPTV / TVRO ANTENNA SEATEL 120TV installed at the following location:       
▪ 
COMPASS DECK STARBOAR 
1.3.2 
ANTENNA CONTROLLER SEATEL LMXP 
 
The Sea Tel LMXP Antenna Controller is installed as part of the onboard satellite TV system supporting the vessel’s 
IPTV distribution network. In this configuration, the controller manages the stabilized satellite antenna that receives 
television broadcast signals, which are then distributed internally through the IPTV system. 
 
The LMXP continuously controls antenna positioning by processing vessel motion data such as heading, roll, and pitch 
to maintain precise alignment with the selected broadcast satellite. This ensures uninterrupted satellite signal 
reception even during vessel movement and rough sea conditions. Once the satellite TV signal is received, it is passed 
to the TVRO and IPTV headend equipment for decoding and distribution across the ship’s internal network to cabins 
and common areas. 
 
The controller provides system monitoring, satellite selection, tracking status, and alarm functions. It also allows 
configuration and diagnostic access to maintain stable performance. By ensuring accurate satellite tracking and 
consistent RF signal quality, the Sea Tel LMXP plays a critical role in maintaining reliable IPTV service onboard. 
 
ANTENNA CONTROLLER SEATEL LMXP installed at the following location:     
▪ 
A-DECK TELECOM ROOM IPTV SYSTEM CABINET (86-RX-009) 
1.3.3 
IPTV SET-TOP BOX CLEARTV CLEAR H98MUV3 
 
The Clear TV Clear H98MUV3 IPTV Set-Top Box is a digital receiver used onboard to decode and display television 
content distributed through the vessel’s IPTV network. It functions as the end-user interface between the ship’s 
internal IP television system and the television display units installed in cabins or common areas. 
The Clear H98MUV3 receives compressed digital video streams transmitted over the vessel’s LAN network from the 
IPTV headend system. It processes and decodes the incoming IP-based television signals and converts them into HDMI 
audio-visual output compatible with standard television screens. The unit supports high-definition video playback and 
provides user navigation through channel selection menus, program guides, and system settings. 
Designed for continuous operation, the set-top box connects to the onboard network via Ethernet and is powered 
locally at each installation point. It ensures stable reception of live TV broadcasts and other IPTV services distributed 
from the satellite TVRO system and IPTV server infrastructure. Within the ship’s communication system, the Clear 
H98MUV3 acts as the final delivery component that enables reliable television viewing for crew and operational areas.

1.3.4 
24P NTEWORK SWITCH HUAWEI S5735-L24T4S-A-V 
The Huawei S5735-L24T4S-A-V is a managed Gigabit Ethernet access switch designed for enterprise and industrial 
network environments. It provides twenty-four 10/100/1000Base-T Ethernet ports for device connectivity along with 
four Gigabit SFP uplink ports for fibre backbone integration. This model supports Layer 2 switching functions with basic 
Layer 3 routing capability, making it suitable for structured LAN architectures where traffic segmentation and 
controlled data distribution are required. 
In an FPSO or offshore installation, the switch typically serves as an access layer device within the communication rack. 
It distributes IP-based services such as IPTV, VoIP, CCTV, automation systems, and general data connectivity to various 
user endpoints. The SFP uplink ports are generally connected to a fibre backbone or core switch, enabling stable high-
bandwidth communication between different decks, control rooms, or E-houses. 
The switch supports VLAN configuration, Quality of Service (QoS), Access Control Lists (ACL), and port security features, 
allowing network segmentation and traffic prioritization according to operational requirements. It also supports IPv4 
and IPv6 dual stack operation and can be managed through a web interface, command line interface (CLI), or SNMP-
based network management systems. 
When installed inside a protected rack cabinet within a controlled communication room, the Huawei S5735-L24T4S-A-
V provides reliable and stable performance suitable for offshore network infrastructure where continuous data 
availability is essential. 
 
24P NTEWORK SWITCH HUAWEI S5735-L24T4S-A-V installed at the following location:     
▪ 
A-DECK TELECOM ROOM IPTV SYSTEM CABINET (86-RX-009) 
1.3.5 
24P NTEWORK SWITCH CISCO C1300-24FP-4X 
 
The Cisco C1300-24FP-4X is a managed Gigabit Ethernet access switch designed for reliable enterprise and industrial 
network applications. It provides 24 Gigabit Ethernet ports with full Power over Ethernet (PoE) capability, allowing 
connected devices such as IP phones, CCTV cameras, wireless access points, and IPTV receivers to receive both data

The TRIAX TMP 5×24 Coaxial Multi-Switch is an RF signal distribution component designed for satellite and terrestrial 
signal distribution systems, providing 5 input feeds and 24 subscriber outputs with integrated power supply 
functionality. It supports combined satellite intermediate frequency (SAT-IF) and terrestrial (TER) signal distribution 
across a wide frequency range typically from 47 MHz to 2150 MHz (terrestrial and satellite bands respectively), 
enabling efficient distribution of broadcast signals to multiple receivers. The unit features a compact, installer-friendly 
design with colour-coded F-connector input and output ports and adjustable gain and slope controls to compensate 
for cable losses and varying subscriber line lengths. It provides low insertion loss, high isolation, and return loss 
performance, ensuring minimal signal degradation between inputs and outputs, and supports standard satellite 
switching commands such as 13 V/18 V and 22 kHz tone control for LNB polarity switching. The Mult switch includes 
an internal switch-mode power supply to feed LNBs where required, and LED indicators for power status, making it 
suitable for medium- to large-scale RF distribution installations in IPTV, MATV, SMATV, or multi-user satellite systems. 
 
COAXIAL MULTI SWITCH TRIAX TMP5X24 installed at the following location:     
▪ 
A-DECK TELECOM ROOM IPTV SYSTEM CABINET (86-RX-009) 
1.3.7 
IPTV SERVER DELL POWEREDGE R740 
 
The Dell PowerEdge R740 is a versatile 2U enterprise rack server engineered to handle compute-intensive and scalable 
workloads including virtualization, VDI, cloud infrastructure, and database applications. It supports up to two Intel 
Xeon Scalable processors and large DDR4 memory capacity, enabling high core density and fast data processing. The 
server offers flexible storage configurations with support for SAS, SATA, and NVMe drives, allowing optimization for 
performance or capacity-focused deployments. 
Designed for modern data centres, the R740 includes multiple PCIe expansion slots for GPU or accelerator support, 
making it suitable for AI inference and high-performance computing scenarios. Integrated iDRAC9 provides advanced 
remote monitoring, automation, and lifecycle management, while redundant power supplies and hot-swappable 
components ensure high availability and reliability. Its balance of scalability, performance, and manageability makes it 
ideal for both enterprise IT environments and edge deployments. 
 
IPTV SERVER installed at the following location:     
▪ 
A-DECK TELECOM ROOM IPTV SYSTEM CABINET (86-RX-009)

1.3.8 
CLEAR ENCODER 4 WAY H3114AV & 8 WAY H3118AV 
 
The Clear is a multi-channel audio/video encoder designed for IPTV headend applications. The unit supports up to 
eight independent AV input channels and converts analog or HDMI video sources into compressed digital IP streams 
suitable for distribution over an Ethernet network. 
Within an FPSO IPTV system, the encoder is typically installed in the communication rack as part of the IPTV headend. 
It receives video inputs from satellite receivers, media players, or other broadcast sources and encodes them into 
MPEG transport streams. These streams are then transmitted via multicast over the LAN to the IPTV server or directly 
to network switches for distribution to set-top boxes and smart TVs. 
The H3118AV supports standard compression formats such as H.264 and generates stable, synchronized IP streams 
optimized for bandwidth efficiency. Each channel operates independently, allowing multiple television programs or 
video sources to be encoded simultaneously. 
This encoder forms a critical interface between traditional AV broadcast equipment and the IP-based distribution 
network, enabling centralized and scalable television services onboard the vessel. 
 
CLEAR ENCODER 4 WAY H3114AV & 8 WAY H3118AV installed at the following location:     
▪ 
A-DECK TELECOM ROOM IPTV SYSTEM CABINET (86-RX-009) 
1.3.9 
KVM ATEN CL5708 
 
The ATEN CL5708 is an integrated LCD KVM (Keyboard, Video, Mouse) console designed to provide centralized control 
of multiple servers from a single rack-mounted unit. It combines a 17-inch LCD monitor, keyboard, touchpad, and an 
8-port KVM switch within a 1U slide-out rack housing, making it suitable for communication and IT cabinets where 
space optimization is essential. 
The CL5708 allows an operator to access and manage up to eight connected servers or network devices directly from 
the front panel console. Each connected device is interfaced using dedicated KVM cables such as the ATEN 2L-5201U, 
which carry video, keyboard, and mouse signals between the console and the target equipment. 
In an FPSO or offshore installation, the KVM console is typically installed in the main communication rack to provide 
local access to IPTV servers, network switches, firewalls, or other IT equipment. This setup eliminates the need for 
separate monitors and keyboards for each device, enabling efficient maintenance, configuration, and troubleshooting 
from a single control point. 
The ATEN CL5708 supports high-resolution video output and secure switching between connected systems, ensuring 
reliable and organized management of critical onboard network infrastructure. 
 
KVM ATEN CL5708 installed at the following location:     
▪ 
A-DECK TELECOM ROOM IPTV SYSTEM CABINET (86-RX-009)

1.3.10 
SMART TV PHILIPS 32HFL3014 
 
The Philips 32HFL3014 is a 32-inch hospitality-grade LED Smart television designed for commercial and marine 
accommodation environments. This model is specifically built for controlled installations such as hotels, offshore 
vessels, and FPSO living quarters where centralized management and reliable operation are required. 
The television supports Full HD resolution and is equipped with multiple input interfaces including HDMI, USB, and RF 
antenna input, allowing connection to IPTV set-top boxes, coaxial TV systems, or media devices. In an FPSO IPTV 
installation, the Philips 32HFL3014 typically operates in conjunction with the onboard IPTV server and set-top box, 
receiving IP-based television streams through the Ethernet network and displaying live TV channels or video-on-
demand content. 
As a hospitality model, the unit includes features such as volume limitation, channel control, cloning capability for bulk 
configuration, and restricted user settings to prevent unauthorized changes. This makes it suitable for crew cabins 
where standardized configuration and ease of maintenance are important. 
The Philips 32HFL3014 forms the end-user display component of the IPTV distribution system, providing reliable 
television service for crew welfare and onboard information broadcasting.

1.3.11 
TV OUTLET SOCKET COMMSCOPE 2153449-4 
 
The CommScope 2153449-4 TV outlet socket is a coaxial wall outlet designed for television signal termination in 
structured cabling systems. It serves as the final connection point between the coaxial distribution network and the 
television or set-top box installed in cabins, lounges, or common areas. 
This outlet is typically used in SMATV or TVRO-based systems where RF television signals are distributed via coaxial 
cable from a centralized headend or satellite receiver. The socket provides a secure and impedance-matched 
connection, ensuring minimal signal loss and maintaining proper signal quality at the user end. 
In an FPSO or offshore installation, the TV outlet socket is mounted in accommodation areas and connected to the 
vessel’s coaxial backbone. It ensures organized termination of RF cables within the wall panel and supports reliable 
television reception as part of the overall IPTV or conventional TV distribution system.

3 
 MANUFACTURERS' MANUALS 
This section includes the manufacturers’ manuals for the installation, operation, and maintenance of the equipment. 
3.1 INSTALLATION INSTRUCTIONS 
3.1.1 
IPTV / TVRO ANTENNA SEATEL 120TV 
The Sea Tel 120 TVHD is a maritime stabilized satellite TVRO antenna system designed to provide reliable satellite 
television reception onboard vessels. The system features a fully enclosed radome antenna with advanced three-axis 
stabilization, enabling continuous satellite tracking and stable signal reception even under vessel motion and rough 
sea conditions. It supports DVB-S and DVB-S2 broadcasts and integrates an automatic satellite acquisition and tracking 
system for uninterrupted TV service. 
The antenna is engineered for marine environments, offering high pointing accuracy and robust mechanical 
construction suitable for continuous operation at sea. With its integrated control and stabilization technology, the Sea 
Tel 120 TVHD ensures dependable onboard entertainment by maintaining consistent satellite lock and high-quality 
signal reception across global coverage areas.

The antenna is engineered for marine environments, offering high pointing accuracy and robust mechanical 
construction suitable for continuous operation at sea. With its integrated control and stabilization technology, the Sea 
Tel 120 TVHD ensures dependable onboard entertainment by maintaining consistent satellite lock and high-quality 
signal reception across global coverage areas.

• 
MOUNTING LOACTION 
 
 
 
 
 
 
 
 
 
 
 
 
 
 
 
 
 
The stabilized TVRO antenna is installed on the Compass Deck at the designated mounting location shown in the layout 
drawing to ensure an unobstructed 360° satellite visibility and minimum shadowing from nearby structures. The 
antenna is mounted on a dedicated steel pole foundation fabricated and installed by the yard, providing sufficient 
elevation above deck level to maintain clear line-of-sight and reduce multipath interference. 
The antenna pedestal is secured to a circular mounting plate using a four-bolt fixing arrangement based on the 
manufacturer’s mounting pattern. The mounting interface incorporates a central cable entry cut-out to allow RF and 
control cables to pass internally through the pole. 
A vertical mounting pole of approximately 100 mm diameter with adequate wall thickness is used to support the 
antenna dome. The RF and control cables are routed internally through the pole and deck sleeve to protect against 
environmental exposure and mechanical damage. A sealed pipe sleeve is installed at the deck penetration point to 
ensure watertight integrity and corrosion protection. 
The mounting arrangement is aligned with vessel bow reference as required by the antenna stabilization system to 
ensure correct azimuth referencing during satellite tracking. All installation practices comply with marine standards 
for vibration resistance, grounding, and corrosion protection, ensuring reliable long-term operation of the TVRO 
system in offshore environments.

written consent of Contractor. This document is issued as an “uncontrolled copy” unless over-stamped “CONTROLLED COPY”. 
 
 
 
The satellite television distribution system is centred around the SEATEL 120TV stabilized antenna, which receives satellite signals 
while compensating for vessel motion. The antenna is electrically and logically controlled by the SEATEL LMXP, which acts as the 
Antenna Control Unit (ACU), providing power, stabilization control, and communication between the antenna pedestal and the 
vessel’s gyro system. 
The SEATEL 120TV antenna delivers four LNB RF outputs corresponding to Vertical High (V/H), Horizontal High (H/H), Horizontal 
Low (H/L), and Vertical Low (V/L) frequency bands. These signals are routed via RG11 coaxial cables to bulkhead connectors and 
then directed toward the distribution system. Simultaneously, the SEATEL LMXP (ACU) supplies DC power, tracking commands, 
and receives feedback signals from the antenna. It interfaces with the vessel’s gyro through a multi-core control cable to obtain 
heading and stabilization data, ensuring accurate satellite tracking during vessel movement. 
3.1.3 
COAXIAL MULTI SWITCH TRIAX TMP5X24

This document is the property of Contractor and should not be distributed, copied, quoted or reproduced in any manner whatsoever, in whole or in part, without the prior 
written consent of Contractor. This document is issued as an “uncontrolled copy” unless over-stamped “CONTROLLED COPY”. 
 
 
From the antenna RF outputs, the four polarization and band signals are connected to the TRIAX TMP5x24. The Multi 
switch receives the complete set of LNB signals and performs band and polarization switching based on control 
voltages sent from individual satellite receivers. By dynamically selecting between Vertical/Horizontal and High/Low 
bands, the Multi switch distributes the appropriate RF signal to each output port. 
Each output of the TRIAX TMP5x24 feeds an individual satellite receiver through RG6 coaxial cable. This configuration 
allows multiple receivers to independently select and view different channels simultaneously without interference. 
The receivers then deliver audio and video signals to the televisions via HDMI connections. 
Overall, the system provides a continuous and structured signal path from the stabilized antenna to end-user television 
points. The SEATEL LMXP ensures accurate antenna tracking and operational control, while the TRIAX TMP5x24 enables 
efficient multi-point RF distribution. The use of RG11 cabling for trunk connections minimizes signal loss over longer 
distances, and RG6 cabling is used for internal distribution and final connections.

From the antenna RF outputs, the four polarization and band signals are connected to the TRIAX TMP5x24. The Multi 
switch receives the complete set of LNB signals and performs band and polarization switching based on control 
voltages sent from individual satellite receivers. By dynamically selecting between Vertical/Horizontal and High/Low 
bands, the Multi switch distributes the appropriate RF signal to each output port. 
Each output of the TRIAX TMP5x24 feeds an individual satellite receiver through RG6 coaxial cable. This configuration 
allows multiple receivers to independently select and view different channels simultaneously without interference. 
The receivers then deliver audio and video signals to the televisions via HDMI connections. 
Overall, the system provides a continuous and structured signal path from the stabilized antenna to end-user television 
points. The SEATEL LMXP ensures accurate antenna tracking and operational control, while the TRIAX TMP5x24 enables 
efficient multi-point RF distribution. The use of RG11 cabling for trunk connections minimizes signal loss over longer 
distances, and RG6 cabling is used for internal distribution and final connections.

This encoder features embedded hardware encoding designed for professional IPTV and video distribution systems. It supports 
multiple input formats including HDMI and CVBS video with independent audio channels, enabling flexible integration with 
cameras, media players, and AV sources. The unit supports HD resolutions up to 1080p and advanced compression formats such 
as H.265 and H.264 profiles, ensuring efficient bandwidth utilization while maintaining high video quality. 
The encoder provides Gigabit Ethernet connectivity with full-duplex transmission and supports multiple streaming protocols 
including RTSP, RTMP, HTTP, and UDP (unicast and multicast). It is compatible with standard streaming servers such as Wowza and 
FMS, allowing seamless live broadcasting and network distribution. Dual-stream functionality enables simultaneous main and sub-
stream output using different protocols and bitrates. 
Additional features include adjustable bitrate control (CBR/VBR), customizable resolution output, on-screen watermark overlay, 
and remote management via web interface. The system supports DHCP network configuration, firmware upgrades, and remote 
maintenance, making it suitable for continuous operation in IPTV, surveillance, and onboard multimedia distribution 
environments.

The coaxial Multi switch functions as the RF distribution hub between the stabilized satellite antenna system and the 
IPTV encoding equipment. It receives multiple RF feeds from the antenna via RG6 coaxial inputs routed through the 
bulkhead connector. These feeds typically represent different polarization and band selections (Vertical/Horizontal 
and High/Low), along with the antenna control (ACU) signal path. 
Inside the cabinet, the Multi switch processes and distributes the incoming satellite IF signals to multiple independent 
RF outputs. Each output provides a selectable satellite feed to a dedicated receiver module associated with the encoder 
channels. This enables simultaneous access to different satellite transponders without signal conflict, allowing multiple 
TV programs to be received in parallel. 
The RF outputs from the Multi switch are connected via coaxial jumpers to the satellite receivers (integrated or 
external), which demodulate the DVB-S/S2 signals and convert them into baseband audio/video outputs. These 
outputs are then fed into the HDMI inputs of the multi-channel encoders. The encoders compress and convert the 
video streams into IP-based transport streams for IPTV distribution over the vessel’s network. 
In summary, the coaxial Multi switch provides controlled RF signal splitting and polarization management, ensuring 
stable multi-channel satellite reception and enabling the encoders to generate multiple IPTV streams from a single 
stabilized antenna source.

The Huawei S5735-L24T4S-A-V is a managed Gigabit Ethernet switch designed for enterprise and industrial access-
layer deployments. It provides 24 × 10/100/1000BASE-T RJ45 ports and 4 × Gigabit SFP uplink ports, enabling high-
speed connectivity between network devices and aggregation layers. The switch is housed in a compact 1U rack-mount 
chassis and supports reliable continuous operation in communication cabinets. 
Based on Huawei’s S5735 series platform, the switch supports advanced Layer 2 features and basic Layer 3 functions 
including VLANs, QoS, static routing, and security controls. It supports flexible management via CLI, web interface, and 
SNMP-based monitoring. In onboard communication systems, the S5735-L24T4S-A-V is typically used as an access or 
distribution switch to interconnect servers, IP communication equipment, and network infrastructure while ensuring 
stable and efficient data transmission.

The Cisco C1300-24FP-4X is a managed Gigabit Ethernet switch designed for enterprise and industrial network 
deployments requiring high port density and PoE capability. It provides 24 x 1-Gigabit RJ45 ports with integrated PoE+ 
power delivery and 4 x 10-Gigabit SFP+ uplink ports for high-speed backbone connectivity. The switch supports a PoE 
power budget of approximately 375 W, enabling reliable operation of IP phones, wireless access points, cameras, and 
other powered network devices.  
Built on the Cisco Catalyst 1300 platform, the unit offers Layer 2 switching features, VLAN segmentation, spanning tree 
protocols, and secure network management functions. With a switching capacity of around 128 Gbps, it supports 
stable traffic forwarding in high-utilization environments.  
In communication or marine systems, the C1300-24FP-4X is typically used as a core or distribution switch to 
interconnect IP radios, servers, IPTV equipment, and network infrastructure while also supplying PoE power to field 
devices.

The CL5708 Slideaway LCD KVM Switch is an integrated control unit that enables centralized management of multiple 
computers from a single keyboard, video, and mouse (KVM) console. A single unit supports control of up to 8 or 16 
computers, and additional switches can be daisy-chained to expand control capacity up to 256 systems from one 
console. 
The device features a space-saving 1U rack-mountable slideways design that integrates a keyboard, touchpad, and 
LED-backlit LCD display. The Full HD display provides clear video output for monitoring and control tasks. For enhanced 
security, the system optionally supports fingerprint authentication to restrict access to both the KVM switch and 
connected servers. 
Built with a modular architecture, the KVM console section can be separated from the switch module, allowing easier 
maintenance, servicing, and component replacement without removing the entire unit from the rack.

The CL5708 Slideaway LCD KVM Switch is an integrated control unit that enables centralized management of multiple 
computers from a single keyboard, video, and mouse (KVM) console. A single unit supports control of up to 8 or 16 
computers, and additional switches can be daisy-chained to expand control capacity up to 256 systems from one 
console. 
The device features a space-saving 1U rack-mountable slideways design that integrates a keyboard, touchpad, and 
LED-backlit LCD display. The Full HD display provides clear video output for monitoring and control tasks. For enhanced 
security, the system optionally supports fingerprint authentication to restrict access to both the KVM switch and 
connected servers. 
Built with a modular architecture, the KVM console section can be separated from the switch module, allowing easier 
maintenance, servicing, and component replacement without removing the entire unit from the rack.

Install the unit using two people: one person should hold the unit in position in the rack while the other secures the 
front brackets. Then slide the left and right L-shaped brackets into the side mounting brackets from the rear and fasten 
them with the provided screws. Once all brackets are aligned, tighten all screws securely. Ensure adequate ventilation 
by leaving at least 5.1 cm clearance on each side and 12.7 cm at the rear for airflow, power, and cable routing. 
 
3.1.9 
DELL POWEREDGE R740 
 
 
 
The Dell PowerEdge R740 is deployed as the central server platform for the IPTV system, providing processing, storage, 
and management functions for onboard television and media distribution services. It operates as the core middleware 
and streaming server, controlling live TV channels, VOD content, user access management, and network-based video 
delivery. 
Built on a dual-processor architecture with scalable memory capacity, the R740 delivers stable performance for 
handling multiple concurrent IPTV streams across the vessel. The server supports flexible storage configurations to 
host media files and system data, with RAID protection ensuring operational reliability and data security.

Integrated high-speed Ethernet connectivity enables seamless integration with IPTV encoders, network switches, and 
smart TVs distributed throughout the FPSO. Redundant power supplies and enterprise-grade hardware design ensure 
continuous 24/7 operation, which is essential for crew welfare services. 
Remote management features allow monitoring, configuration, and troubleshooting without direct physical access, 
supporting efficient maintenance in offshore environments. Overall, the PowerEdge R740 provides a reliable and 
scalable platform for delivering consistent IPTV services onboard.

Following RF distribution by the coaxial Mult switch, the satellite IF outputs are routed to multiple satellite receiver 
modules associated with the IPTV encoding chain. Each receiver converts the selected DVB-S/S2 RF feed into baseband 
audio/video signals. These baseband outputs are then connected via HDMI cables to the multi-channel hardware 
encoders, including the 4-channel and 8-channel models (CLEAR-H3118AV and CLEAR-H311AV). The encoders perform 
real-time compression of the incoming audio/video signals using H.264/H.265 encoding and convert them into IP-
based transport streams suitable for IPTV distribution. 
Each encoder provides multiple Ethernet outputs carrying encoded IP streams. These outputs are terminated using 
Cat6 patch cords and connected to the primary aggregation switch, Switch-1 (24-port Huawei S5735-L24T4S-A-V). This 
switch acts as the central IPTV distribution node, handling multicast/unicast streaming traffic and integrating the IPTV 
subsystem into the vessel’s wider IP infrastructure. 
From Switch-1, the encoded IPTV streams are distributed across the shipboard LAN. The switch is also interconnected 
with the KVM console system (ATEN CL5708), allowing centralized monitoring and control of the IPTV server and 
associated hardware from a single rack-mounted console. The Dell PowerEdge R740 server is connected to the same 
switch and functions as the IPTV middleware and content server, providing channel management, VOD services, and

system administration. The server also interfaces with other IP-based services such as CCTV and general LAN 
connectivity, enabling unified network management over the same backbone. 
For network expansion and redundancy, Switch-1 is up linked to a secondary distribution switch, Switch-2 (Cisco 
C1300-24FP-4X), via high-speed Ethernet uplink ports. This inter-switch link extends IPTV and data services to network 
segments, supporting wider coverage across accommodation and operational areas. The Cisco switch also provides 
Power-over-Ethernet and additional port density for Smart TVs, IPTV set-top boxes, and auxiliary IP devices. 
Overall, the signal flow progresses from RF satellite reception (Mult switch) to HDMI-based encoding, then into IP 
streaming via the Huawei aggregation switch. The network core interconnects with servers, KVM management, CCTV 
systems, and downstream access switches, forming a fully integrated IPTV and IP distribution architecture suitable for 
marine deployment.

The illustrated rack layout shows the IPTV headend equipment installed inside a floor-standing 19-inch cabinet 
designed for centralized onboard media distribution. The cabinet integrates all active and passive components 
required for satellite TV reception, IPTV processing, and network distribution within a structured and serviceable rack 
arrangement. 
At the top of the cabinet, fibre optic and copper patch panels are installed together with horizontal cable management 
units to maintain structured routing and segregation of signal paths. Dedicated IPTV patch panels and network 
switches are arranged in the upper sections to provide organized interconnection between encoders, server, and 
vessel LAN infrastructure. Blank panels are used to maintain airflow control and allow space for future expansion. 
The mid-section of the rack houses the SeaTel antenna control interface, KVM switch, and IPTV server platform, 
enabling centralized monitoring and control of the headend system. Below this, multiple satellite TV receivers are 
installed in stacked configuration, providing multi-channel satellite signal demodulation for redistribution across the 
IPTV system. 
The lower portion of the cabinet contains video encoding equipment, including multi-channel HD encoders that 
convert receiver outputs into IP streams. Additional network switches are installed to aggregate encoded streams and 
distribute them across the onboard network. Structured cable management panels are placed between active devices 
to ensure proper routing, airflow, and maintenance accessibility. 
Power distribution and protection equipment are installed at the bottom section within an integrated MCB enclosure, 
providing controlled AC supply to all rack-mounted devices. The cabinet is mounted on a raised base plinth for 
mechanical stability and cable entry clearance. 
 
3.1.11 
TV OUTLET SOCKET

The CommScope 760249251 faceplate is installed at the wall outlet location to provide a structured termination point 
for IPTV and TV signal connections. It accommodates modular keystone inserts, including RJ45 data jacks and TV outlet 
modules, ensuring a clean and secure interface for end-user connectivity. 
The CommScope 2153449-4 TV outlet module is fitted into the faceplate and connected internally to the coaxial cable 
routed from the patch panel. As shown in the installation diagram, the RJ45 keystone jack connects to the structured 
cabling system, while the coaxial outlet connects to the set-top box or television equipment. 
Together, the faceplate and outlet module provide a professional wall-mounted termination solution, ensuring 
organized cabling, reliable signal transmission, and easy maintenance within the IPTV distribution system.

The Clear H98MUV3 IPTV set-top box is a compact Android-based media receiver designed for IPTV and VOD service 
delivery in marine or enterprise environments. It functions as the end-user decoding device within the IPTV system, 
receiving multicast or unicast IP video streams from the IPTV server over the local network and converting them into 
HDMI output for display on televisions. 
The unit supports high-definition video playback, including H.264/H.265 formats, ensuring smooth decoding of live TV 
channels and on-demand content. It connects to the network via an Ethernet interface and integrates easily with 
structured cabling systems using RJ45 outlets. The HDMI output provides direct connection to smart TVs or standard 
displays, while USB ports allow firmware updates or local media playback if required. 
In an FPSO or offshore installation, the H98MUV3 operates as the subscriber endpoint of the IPTV distribution system, 
enabling crew access to live satellite TV, internal broadcast channels, and VOD services with stable performance and 
simple plug-and-play operation 
 
 
 
 
 
 
 
 
 
 
 
 
 
 
 
 
 
 
 
 
 
 
The IPTV set-top box is wall-mounted using a dedicated steel mounting bracket as shown in the installation detail. The 
bracket is fabricated in an L-shape profile and secured to the bulkhead using four M6 bolts, ensuring firm mechanical

The SMART TV is installed using a heavy-duty wall-mounted articulating bracket suitable for marine environments. A 
steel mounting plate is first welded or securely fixed to the metal bulkhead, ensuring a rigid and vibration-resistant 
foundation. The wall plate of the TV bracket is then aligned with the mounting plate and secured using two M6 bolts 
and nuts through the pre-drilled holes, as indicated in Detail-A. 
Once the bracket base is firmly fixed, the articulated arm assembly is attached and tightened to eliminate unwanted 
movement. The TV mounting interface is fixed to the rear of the SMART TV using manufacturer-supplied screws, 
ensuring proper load distribution. The television is then hooked or bolted onto the bracket arm and mechanically 
locked in position. 
After mechanical installation, HDMI and power cables are routed with adequate slack to allow arm movement while 
preventing strain on connectors. The articulating arm allows viewing angle adjustment and facilitates maintenance 
access. All fasteners are tightened and checked to ensure the installation remains secure under vibration and vessel 
motion conditions. 
 
 
The SMART TV wiring is completed by connecting the HDMI output from the IPTV set-top box to the HDMI input located 
on the TV rear interface panel. Power is supplied via a 13A AC plug connected to the nearest utility socket. If required, 
network or service connections can be accessed through the side panel ports. 
All cables are routed neatly along the bracket and secured to avoid strain during TV movement. Adequate slack is 
maintained to allow articulation while ensuring connectors remain firmly seated. Wiring is dressed and tied to prevent 
interference, mechanical damage, or loosening due to vessel vibration.

3.1.14 
IP TV DISTRIBUTION 
 
 
 
 
 
 
 
 
 
 
 
 
 
 
 
 
 
 
 
 
 
 
 
 
 
 
 
The IPTV distribution system delivers TV content from the central IPTV system cabinet in the Telecom Room to multiple TVs located 
across different decks and rooms. Video streams are transmitted over the IP network using structured CAT6 cabling via network 
switches, ensuring stable and high-quality signal delivery. Each TV point is terminated at a local data outlet and connected through 
HDMI to the display, allowing centralized content management, monitoring, and easy expansion of the IPTV services throughout 
the vessel.

3.2 OPERATION – IP TV SYSTEM 
3.2.1 
OPERATION MANUAL AND CONFIGURATION FOR ANTENNA CONTROLLER SEATEL LMXP 
 
The first thing you need to do is to configure your computer so that it will display the MXP screens. Follow these 
instructions to accomplish that. 
 
• 
Initial Network Connection 
 
▪ 
Connect an Ethernet (LAN) cable to the network port of the computer. If connecting through 
the vessel LAN instead of directly to a single PC, ensure the LAN router/switch is linked to the 
LMXP. 
▪ 
Connect the other end of the LAN cable to the Ethernet port on the rear of the LMXP. 
▪ 
Power ON the LMXP and allow the unit to complete start-up. 
▪ 
From the computer desktop, open the Control Panel. 
▪ 
Select “View network status and tasks.” 
• 
Network Adapter Settings 
 
▪ 
Click “Change adapter settings.” 
▪ 
Select “Local Area Connection.” 
▪ 
Click “Properties.” 
▪ 
Double-click “Internet Protocol Version 4 (IPv4).” 
▪ 
Select “Use the following IP address.” 
• 
IP Configuration 
 
▪ 
In the IP address field, enter 10.1.1.102 for the computer. 
▪ 
Note: Alternative addresses such as 10.1.1.101, 10.1.1.103, etc. may be used, provided they 
do not match the LMXP default address (10.1.1.100). 
▪ 
Enter the Subnet Mask as 255.255.255.0. 
▪ 
Click “OK” to save the IPv4 configuration. 
▪ 
In the Local Area Connection Properties window, click “OK.” 
▪ 
Click “Close.” 
▪ 
Close the Control Panel window. 
 
• 
Accessing the LMXP Interface 
 
▪ 
Launch a web browser and enter the following address in the URL bar: http://10.1.1.100 
▪ 
At the login screen, enter the appropriate username (Dealer, Sys Admin, or User). The 
password is issued by Cobham/Sea Tel service support.

Quick Start Operation 
 
Provided the system configuration is correct and the vessel has not moved since the last operation, the antenna will 
automatically perform satellite acquisition following a cold start. Upon successful acquisition, the modem will achieve 
lock, enabling normal service operation. 
 
• 
Turn Power ON 
▪ 
Switch ON the LMXP and the antenna pedestal by setting the front-panel power switch on the 
LMXP to the ON position. 
 
 
 
▪ 
Power ON all other below-decks equipment, including satellite receivers, distribution units, 
and audio/video devices. 
▪ 
The antenna system will start up, complete its initialization sequence, and then automatically 
retarget the last satellite that was previously in use. 
 
If satellite signal is found AND network lock is achieved: 
 
▪ 
The system will automatically enter tracking mode (the Tracking LED on the front panel will 
light up) and the antenna will optimize its pointing to achieve the strongest possible received 
signal from the selected satellite. 
 
 
▪ 
When the received signal level is above the configured threshold and the modem successfully 
establishes network lock, the antenna will continue to follow the satellite without user 
intervention.

▪ 
The configured satellite name (if available), tracking status, modem lock status, and signal 
strength (both numeric readout and bar indicator) will be displayed on the top banner of the 
LMXP graphical interface. 
 
Once these conditions are satisfied, the system will remain in normal automatic operation until any of the following 
events occur: 
▪ 
System AC power is switched off or lost, or 
▪ 
The satellite line-of-sight becomes obstructed, or 
▪ 
The vessel enters a coverage area with insufficient satellite signal strength. 
 
To Target a different satellite: 
 
▪ 
Log in to the LMXP graphical user interface (GUI). 
▪ 
To switch to another satellite, open the Satellite Search – Auto page and choose the required 
satellite from the drop-down menu. 
▪ 
After selecting the satellite, a temporary status message will appear: “Acquiring satellite 
signal… Please wait.” 
▪ 
Once acquisition is successful, the status message will update to indicate: “Satellite signal 
detected. Lock: ON.” 
 
Front Panel Status LEDs 
 
 
 
The basic description of the front panel LED states are: 
 
ERROR - ON [Solid or Flashing Red OR Solid or Flashing Amber] indicates that one, or more, discrete system errors have 
occurred. Refer to Status – Error Code information menu to determine which error(s) have occurred.  
OFF indicates that no errors have occurred 
 
INIT (Initializing) - (Solid Green) indicates that the Antenna is initializing. Initialization of the antenna will take 
approximately two minutes. (Flashing Amber) indicates that a software update in progress, the system (ADE-BDE) is 
synchronizing or in service/out of service testing is in progress. 
 
TARGET -ON (Solid Green) indicates that the antenna is TARGETING (driving) to the specified Azimuth and/or Elevation 
position(s). 
 
SEARCH (Searching) - ON (Solid Green) indicates that LMXP is actively searching for your satellite signal. OFF indicates 
that SEARCH is OFF.

TRACK (Tracking) - ON (Solid Green) indicates that the LMXP has identified and is actively tracking the desired satellite 
to optimize the signal level (AGC). Blinking indicates that the LMXP is in search delay or that the system is analyzing a 
satellite signal. OFF indicates that Tracking is OFF. 
 
BLOCK - ON (Solid Red) indicates that the antenna is within a defined blockage zone (therefore you should not have 
satellite signal until the antenna is no longer within the blockage zone). OFF indicates that the antenna is not within a 
defined blockage zone). If no blockage zones have been entered, the antenna could be blocked causing loss of signal. 
 
LINK - (Green LED) ON (Solid Green) indicates that the LMXP has good communications with the antenna (therefore 
good antenna control). This is normal state for system operation. ON (Solid Red) indicates LOSS of communications 
with the antenna (therefore, loss of antenna control). Check the coax connections on the LMXP and in the base of the 
radome. 
 
STATUS - (Green LED) ON (Solid Red) indicates that the signal is below threshold (satellite signal is low or lost). OFF 
indicates that status is normal. 
 
POWER - (Green LED) OFF indicates that LMXP Power switch is OFF (No power to the LMXP or the antenna). ON 
(Flashing Green) indicates that the LMXP is booting up. ON (Solid Green) indicates that the LMXP boot sequence is 
complete and the system power is ON (normal). 
 
3.2.2 
BASIC OPERATION – IPTV SYSTEM 
• 
System Power-Up Sequence 
▪ 
Ensure the LMXP and antenna system are powered ON and tracking the selected satellite with 
stable signal. 
▪ 
Power ON the coaxial Mult switch and verify satellite IF inputs are active. 
▪ 
Power ON the IPTV encoders (CLEAR-H3118AV / CLEAR-H311AV). 
▪ 
Power ON the IP network switches (Huawei S5735-L24T4S-A-V and Cisco C1300-24FP-4X). 
▪ 
Power ON the IPTV server (Dell PowerEdge R740) and confirm the IPTV application/services 
are running. 
▪ 
Power ON IPTV Set-Top Boxes (STBs) and/or Smart TVs at user locations. 
• 
Signal Flow Check 
▪ 
Satellite RF from antenna → Coaxial Multi switch 
▪ 
Multi switch IF outputs → CLEAR Encoders (HDMI/AV inputs) 
▪ 
Encoders IP output → Huawei Switch (Core) → Cisco Switch (Access) 
▪ 
Network → IPTV Server → Smart TVs 
 
Make sure Encoder channel LEDs/status show streaming active. Network switch ports show link/activity. IPTV server 
shows channels online. 
 
• 
IPTV Channel Availability 
On the IPTV server, verify that all encoder inputs are correctly mapped to the corresponding channels and that channel 
names and numbers are properly published. From the Smart TV, refresh or reload the channel list. Select a channel 
and confirm that video is displayed correctly, audio is clear, and there is no excessive buffering or freezing.

3.3 MAINTENANCE AND TROUBLESHOOTING 
 
The IPTV system requires minimal routine maintenance during normal operation. Periodic visual inspections shall be 
carried out to ensure that all equipment, including the satellite antenna system, coaxial multi switch, IPTV encoders, 
network switches, IPTV server, and end-user devices (Smart TVs), are powered ON, free from physical damage, and 
operating within normal status indications. Network connectivity and streaming performance should be verified at 
regular intervals by checking selected channels for correct video and audio quality without excessive buffering or 
interruptions. Cooling fans, ventilation paths, and rack-mounted equipment shall be kept clean and unobstructed to 
prevent overheating. Firmware and software updates for the LMXP, encoders, IPTV server, and network switches 
should be performed as per manufacturer recommendations and vessel IT change management procedures. Any 
abnormal alarms, signal degradation, or service interruptions shall be recorded and investigated promptly, and 
corrective actions shall be carried out by qualified technical personnel. 
3.3.1 
MAINTENANCE OF IPTV SYSTEM 
 
Routine preventive maintenance shall be carried out to ensure continuous and reliable operation of the IPTV and TVRO 
antenna system. Shipboard personnel may perform basic inspections and functional checks; however, internal 
servicing and calibration of the antenna pedestal and LMXP controller shall be performed only by authorized service 
personnel. 
TVRO Antenna – Sea Tel 120TV 
• 
Perform periodic visual inspection of the radome to check for cracks, physical damage, or water ingress. 
• 
Clean the radome surface regularly to remove salt deposits and oil residues. 
• 
Inspect the antenna mounting base, pedestal, and fasteners to ensure they remain secure and free from 
abnormal vibration. 
• 
Check coaxial cables, cable glands, and grounding connections for tightness, corrosion, and mechanical 
damage. 
• 
Monitor antenna tracking performance, signal level, and stabilization status via the LMXP GUI to confirm stable 
satellite lock during normal operation. 
 
Antenna Controller – Sea Tel LMXP 
• 
Verify power status and front-panel LED indications during routine checks. 
• 
Ensure adequate ventilation around the LMXP and keep air inlets/outlets free from obstruction to prevent 
overheating. 
• 
Inspect Ethernet, coaxial, and power connections at the rear panel for secure termination and proper strain 
relief. 
• 
Back up configuration files after commissioning and following any configuration changes. 
• 
Perform firmware/software updates only in accordance with manufacturer recommendations and vessel IT 
change management procedures. 
 
IPTV System 
• 
Check operational status of IPTV encoders, IPTV server, and network switches, and confirm normal link/activity 
indications on switch ports.

3.3.2 
TROUBLESHOOTING GUIDE 
 
LMXP (No Satellite Acquisition After Cold Start) 
 
Step 
Observation / Check 
Expected Indication 
Corrective Action 
1 
Front panel LEDs 
during startup 
Tracking LED flashes briefly, 
followed by Search LED ON 
This indicates the system has entered satellite search 
mode. Allow the search cycle to continue. 
2 
Antenna search 
behaviour 
Antenna moves in a programmed 
search pattern 
Observe the signal bar graph on the LMXP GUI. Red bars 
indicate signal level is below the threshold. 
3 
Signal bar graph status 
Bar graph remains red (signal 
below threshold) 
This confirms that no valid satellite signal has been 
detected during the current search sweep. 
4 
Repeated search cycles 
Antenna retargets and repeats the 
search sequence 
Allow the system to continue searching until the satellite 
is acquired or operator intervention is required. 
5 
Operator access 
Log in to the LMXP GUI 
Proceed with manual verification and configuration 
checks. 
6 
Communication 
interface settings 
Navigate to Configuration → 
Communication Interfaces 
Access position and heading parameters for verification. 
7 
Vessel position data 
Latitude, Longitude, and Heading 
values displayed 
If values are correct, proceed to Step 10. 
8 
Position correction 
Incorrect Latitude/Longitude 
Enter the correct vessel Latitude and Longitude values 
and apply the changes. 
9 
Heading correction 
Incorrect Heading value 
Enter the correct heading value and apply the changes. 
10 
Satellite selection page 
Open Satellite Search – Auto page 
Prepare to verify the selected satellite. 
11 
Satellite selection 
Correct satellite selected 
If correct, proceed to Step 14. 
12 
Satellite re-selection 
Incorrect satellite selected 
Select the required satellite from the drop-down list. 
13 
Save configuration 
Configuration changes applied 
Click Save to store the updated settings. 
14 
Line-of-sight check 
Possible physical blockage 
Inspect for obstructions (masts, cranes, structures). 
Blockage is the most common cause of acquisition 
failure. 
15 
Cable inspection 
Loose or disconnected cables 
Verify all RF, control, and power cable connections 
between ADE and BDE are secure. 
16 
Escalation 
System still not acquiring satellite 
If the antenna is unobstructed and all connections are 
confirmed, contact the authorized dealer or service 
support.

written consent of Contractor. This document is issued as an “uncontrolled copy” unless over-stamped “CONTROLLED COPY”. 
• 
Periodically verify selected TV channels to ensure correct video and clear audio without freezing, pixelation, 
or excessive buffering. 
• 
Inspect rack-mounted equipment, cooling fans, and power distribution units, and keep ventilation paths clean 
and unobstructed. 
• 
Monitor network performance to ensure stable streaming across the vessel. 
 
3.3.2 
TROUBLESHOOTING GUIDE 
 
LMXP (No Satellite Acquisition After Cold Start) 
 
Step 
Observation / Check 
Expected Indication 
Corrective Action 
1 
Front panel LEDs 
during startup 
Tracking LED flashes briefly, 
followed by Search LED ON 
This indicates the system has entered satellite search 
mode. Allow the search cycle to continue. 
2 
Antenna search 
behaviour 
Antenna moves in a programmed 
search pattern 
Observe the signal bar graph on the LMXP GUI. Red bars 
indicate signal level is below the threshold. 
3 
Signal bar graph status 
Bar graph remains red (signal 
below threshold) 
This confirms that no valid satellite signal has been 
detected during the current search sweep. 
4 
Repeated search cycles 
Antenna retargets and repeats the 
search sequence 
Allow the system to continue searching until the satellite 
is acquired or operator intervention is required. 
5 
Operator access 
Log in to the LMXP GUI 
Proceed with manual verification and configuration 
checks. 
6 
Communication 
interface settings 
Navigate to Configuration → 
Communication Interfaces 
Access position and heading parameters for verification. 
7 
Vessel position data 
Latitude, Longitude, and Heading 
values displayed 
If values are correct, proceed to Step 10. 
8 
Position correction 
Incorrect Latitude/Longitude 
Enter the correct vessel Latitude and Longitude values 
and apply the changes. 
9 
Heading correction 
Incorrect Heading value 
Enter the correct heading value and apply the changes. 
10 
Satellite selection page 
Open Satellite Search – Auto page 
Prepare to verify the selected satellite. 
11 
Satellite selection 
Correct satellite selected 
If correct, proceed to Step 14. 
12 
Satellite re-selection 
Incorrect satellite selected 
Select the required satellite from the drop-down list. 
13 
Save configuration 
Configuration changes applied 
Click Save to store the updated settings. 
14 
Line-of-sight check 
Possible physical blockage 
Inspect for obstructions (masts, cranes, structures). 
Blockage is the most common cause of acquisition 
failure. 
15 
Cable inspection 
Loose or disconnected cables 
Verify all RF, control, and power cable connections 
between ADE and BDE are secure. 
16 
Escalation 
System still not acquiring satellite 
If the antenna is unobstructed and all connections are 
confirmed, contact the authorized dealer or service 
support.
